one_line_takeaway: A white-box alternating-GCG attack crafts 'Super Suffixes' that fool an LLM's guard model very reliably (0.93-0.96 benign) while jailbreaking the generator less reliably, and the authors' DeltaGuard time-series detector catches them by tracking how residual-stream similarity to the refusal direction evolves across tokens.
key_finding: Guard evasion is ROBUST and uniform: Super Suffixes push Prompt Guard 2's average benign score to 0.93-0.96 on malicious-code prompts and 0.86-0.91 on HarmBench across all five generators, up from 0.29-0.52 for primary suffixes alone. Alignment-breaking is WEAKER and domain/model-dependent: on the code dataset the Super-Suffix refusal rate falls to 0.05-0.44 (e.g. Gemma 0.97 -> 0.35), but on HarmBench it stays at 0.55-0.62, meaning the underlying jailbreak only lands ~40-45% of the time. So the honest summary is: they reliably fool the guard, and breaking the target model's alignment is the softer, less consistent result. Appending the secondary suffix slightly WEAKENS the primary jailbreak (a documented trade-off). A notable ancillary finding: Prompt Guard 2 rates BARE malicious prompts (no suffix) as 76% benign — it fails on plain harmful content because it is scoped to jailbreak/injection detection, not content moderation; this gap motivates DeltaGuard. DeltaGuard flips Super-Suffix prompts back to non-benign, assigning them at most ~7% average benign probability (vs. the guard's >90%), and reportedly raises non-benign classification to nearly 100%; it also distinguishes primary from Super suffixes (Gemma confusion matrix: 55/65 Super, 76/82 primary correct).
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'Super Suffixes': adversarial suffixes that simultaneously (a) break a text-generation model's alignment and (b) fool the separate guard model that screens the input. The construction is two-stage. A PRIMARY suffix jailbreaks the generator using a GCG-style search steered toward a refusal/malicious-code concept direction (an approach the authors describe as 'similar to IRIS' [Huang et al. 33], which itself builds on GCG [Zou et al. 32] and the single refusal direction of Arditi et al. [38] — these are prior work, not the paper's invention). The GENUINELY NOVEL piece is a SECONDARY suffix produced by a joint/alternating optimization ('Alternating GCG', Algorithm 1) that co-optimizes a text-gen loss and a guard-model loss even though the two models use DIFFERENT tokenizers, which makes a single combined-gradient step infeasible. They sidestep this by alternating the linear-approximation target between the two models every N=5 iterations and evaluating candidates on a weighted combined loss (Eq. 4). The authors claim this is the first work to jointly bypass alignment AND the guard model (Llama Prompt Guard 2 86M) simultaneously; prior guard-bypass work [44,45] fooled only the guard, not both. Secondary contributions: a new 99-malicious / 99-benign malicious-code-generation dataset, and DeltaGuard, a countermeasure. DeltaGuard's underlying signal (cosine similarity of residual stream to a refusal direction) is NOT new — JBShield [Zhang et al. 50] already used cosine-to-refusal for jailbreak detection. DeltaGuard's specific novelty is treating the TIME SERIES of that similarity across token positions (before/after the generation-start token) as a KNN-classified fingerprint, rather than JBShield's single-point read.
threat_model: A text-generation model has ingested hazardous content and can regenerate it; a smaller guard model (Llama Prompt Guard 2 86M) classifies each incoming prompt as benign/malicious and the response is returned only if labeled benign. The attacker appends a Super Suffix to a malicious request to defeat both. Suffix CRAFTING is white-box: it requires gradients, residual-stream access, and refusal/concept directions for BOTH the generator and the guard model — feasible here only because every target (Gemma 2B, Vicuna 7B, Llama 3.1 8B, Llama 3.2 3B, Phi-3-mini) and the guard are open-weight; the paper does not demonstrate transfer to closed frontier models. DELIVERY can be black-box: the attacker directly queries the deployed model as an ordinary user, or poisons public content (social-media comments, product sites, code repositories) or corporate/RAG databases with innocuous-looking prompt modifiers (Section 4.1). DeltaGuard is a DEPLOYER-SIDE defense that also needs model internals, not a drop-in third-party filter.
limitations: (1) Suffix crafting is white-box and demonstrated only on open-weight models; no evidence of transfer to closed frontier models. (2) The attack is computationally intensive — a full 300-iteration search is expended even when it fails to find a Super Suffix — and primary suffixes are 'fragile' (secondary suffix degrades the jailbreak). (3) Alignment-breaking is inconsistent, especially on HarmBench (refusal still 0.55-0.62). (4) DeltaGuard requires deployer-side access to model internals and a per-model refusal direction; detection-layer choice materially affects accuracy (layer 16 vs. 26). (5) The authors explicitly concede DeltaGuard itself could be jointly bypassed with the same alternating-optimization strategy (defining a combined loss over DeltaGuard + guard). (6) DeltaGuard evaluation uses only an 80/20 split of the paper's own generated suffixes, not held-out attack families. (7) Code and data are not yet released (pending internal review), limiting reproducibility. (8) Minor internal inconsistencies exist between narrative text and tables (e.g. Vicuna code-gen baseline 0.35 in Table 3 vs. '37%' in text).

datasets_used: New Malicious Code Generation dataset (authors' own): 99 malicious + 99 benign prompts across 5 categories (Malware, Spyware, Network Security, Hardware Security, Application Security)
HarmBench (100 randomly selected malicious prompts) [Mazeika et al. 19]
allenai/wildguard used as the harmful-output evaluator model [55]
quantitative_results: Super Suffix guard benign scores (malicious code): 0.94 Gemma, 0.93 Vicuna, 0.96 Llama3.1, 0.93 Llama3.2, 0.93 Phi (Table 3)
Primary suffix guard benign scores (malicious code): 0.43 Gemma, 0.45 Vicuna, 0.52 Llama3.1, 0.42 Llama3.2, 0.29 Phi (Table 3)
Refusal rate malicious code, no suffix -> primary -> Super: Gemma 0.97->0.13->0.35; Vicuna 0.35->0.00->0.05; Llama3.1 0.78->0.12->0.38; Llama3.2 0.86->0.19->0.42; Phi 0.96->0.21->0.44 (Table 3)
HarmBench Super Suffix guard benign scores: 0.86-0.91; Super Suffix refusal rates 0.55-0.62 (Table 4)
DeltaGuard vs Prompt Guard benign prob on Super Suffixes: DeltaGuard 0.01 Gemma, 0.07 Vicuna, 0.00 Llama3.1, 0.07 Llama3.2, 0.05 Phi vs guard 0.93-0.96 (Table 6)
Prompt Guard rates bare malicious prompts (no suffix) 76% benign vs DeltaGuard 0.00-0.03 (Table 6)
Best refusal-vector configuration: direction from layer 13 applied at layer 15 -> 27/29 successful primary suffixes (Table 2)
Gemma DeltaGuard 4-class confusion matrix: 55/65 Super Suffixes and 76/82 primary suffixes correctly classified (Figure 5)
Cost/time per suffix: Vicuna 9 min/$0.23, Llama3.1 36 min/$0.90, Gemma 41 min/$1.02, Llama3.2 45 min/$1.11, Phi 85 min/$2.12 (Table 5)
Attack params: up to 300 GCG iterations, 3 tokens/iteration; alternation window N=5; guard threshold tau_guard=0.85; DeltaGuard window B=4/A=4, KNN k=3; ~700 GPU-hours total, ~75.6 kgCO2eq
baselines_compared: Llama Prompt Guard 2 86M (the guard model being attacked and the detection baseline compared against DeltaGuard)
Primary-suffix-only attack (GCG steered by concept direction, IRIS-style [33]) as the pre-joint-optimization baseline
JBShield [Zhang et al. 50] (conceptually — single-point cosine-to-refusal detector that DeltaGuard's time-series approach extends)
Five target generators: google/gemma-2b-it, lmsys/vicuna-7b-v1.5, meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-instruct, meta-llama/Llama-3.2-3B-instruct, microsoft/Phi-3-mini-128k-instruct
